sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A behavioral-health app tested three reinforcement schedules for building exercise habits. Participants earned points for completing 20-minute walks. One group received points after every walk, a second received points after a variable number of walks, and a third received only weekly educational tips without points. After 6 weeks, average walks per week were 3.8, 4.6, and 2.1. When points were removed during week 7, walks fell to 2.7 in the continuous-reinforcement group and 4.1 in the variable-ratio group. App designers concluded that variable reinforcement produced more persistent behavior after rewards stopped.
questions.stem: Which group showed the most persistent walking after points were removed?

choices.D: All groups showed the same persistence

explanations.C: Correct. The variable-ratio group still averaged 4.1 walks after points were removed, higher than the continuous-reinforcement group at 2.7. The education-only group was not described as having points removed because it did not receive them. The key comparison is persistence after reinforcement ended.
explanations.A: The continuous group declined more sharply when points were removed.
explanations.B: The education-only group had the lowest walking during the program and was not the reward-removal comparison.
explanations.D: The reported week-7 values were not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Average weekly walks during reinforcement and after points were removed.

questions.stem: Why are variable-ratio schedules often resistant to extinction?

choices.A: The reward is unpredictable, so behavior can persist despite missed rewards
choices.B: The behavior is never learned in the first place
choices.C: The reward is delivered after every response forever

explanations.A: Correct. Under variable-ratio schedules, individuals learn that reinforcement does not follow every response, so a period without reward is not a clear signal that rewards are gone. That makes the behavior more resistant to extinction than continuously reinforced behavior.
explanations.B: The high walking rate shows the behavior was learned.
explanations.C: Reward after every response describes continuous reinforcement, not variable ratio.
explanations.D: The schedule maintained high walking rather than removing motivation.

questions.stem: What happened to the continuous-reinforcement group after rewards stopped?

choices.A: Walking increased above the variable-ratio group
choices.B: Walking stayed exactly at 3.8 per week
choices.C: Participants were no longer able to walk

explanations.D: Correct. The continuous group dropped from 3.8 walks during the program to 2.7 after points were removed. That decline is consistent with extinction being faster after a behavior has been reinforced every time.
explanations.A: It remained lower than the variable-ratio group after reward removal.
explanations.B: The value changed from 3.8 to 2.7, so it did not stay the same.
explanations.C: The passage concerns motivation and behavior frequency, not physical inability.

questions.stem: An app designer applying these findings should most likely do what?

choices.B: Use intermittent rewards while also building intrinsic and practical supports
choices.A: Rely only on weekly educational tips
choices.C: Remove all feedback from the app
choices.D: Give rewards for unrelated behaviors only

explanations.B: Correct. The variable-ratio schedule produced the strongest persistence, so intermittent reinforcement can be useful. A strong design would combine it with other supports so users do not depend entirely on points.
explanations.A: Education-only tips produced fewer walks than both reinforcement conditions.
explanations.C: Removing feedback ignores the reinforcement pattern that increased walking.
explanations.D: Rewards need to follow the target behavior to shape that behavior.

questions.stem: Which limitation should be considered before generalizing the finding?

choices.C: A 7-week app study may not predict exercise habits months or years later
choices.A: The study did not include any behavior measure
choices.B: Walking cannot be influenced by reinforcement
choices.D: The variable-ratio group had no outcome data

explanations.C: Correct. The results show short-term persistence after reward removal, but long-term maintenance may depend on identity, environment, health, and intrinsic motivation. The study is useful but does not prove permanent habit change.
explanations.A: Walks per week were directly measured.
explanations.B: The group differences show walking behavior can be influenced by reinforcement schedules.
explanations.D: The variable-ratio group had both program and week-7 outcome data.

questions.passage: A substance-use research team tested a brief craving-management exercise for adults trying to reduce nicotine vaping. Participants completed either urge-surfing mindfulness practice, a neutral reading task, or a budgeting exercise unrelated to vaping. They then made choices between smaller immediate payments and larger delayed payments, and rated vaping urge on a 1 to 10 scale. Immediate-reward choices occurred on 48%, 62%, and 59% of trials. Mean urge ratings were 4.9, 6.8, and 6.2. The mindfulness group reported noticing urges without acting on them.
questions.stem: Which group showed the lowest immediate-reward choice rate?

choices.C: All groups chose immediate rewards equally often

explanations.D: Correct. The mindfulness group chose the immediate reward on 48% of trials, lower than the neutral reading group at 62% and the budgeting group at 59%. That pattern suggests less delay discounting after the urge-surfing exercise.
explanations.A: The neutral reading group had the highest immediate-choice rate.
explanations.B: The budgeting group was lower than neutral reading but still higher than mindfulness.
explanations.C: The values were 48%, 62%, and 59%, so the groups were not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Immediate-reward choices and urge ratings after three brief tasks.

questions.stem: Choosing smaller immediate rewards over larger delayed rewards is called what?

explanations.A: Correct. Delay discounting refers to devaluing rewards as they become more delayed, often reflected by choosing a smaller immediate option over a larger later one. The task in the passage directly measures that tendency.
explanations.B: Stimulus generalization is a learning process in which responses transfer to similar stimuli.
explanations.C: Role strain concerns tension within a social role, not intertemporal reward choice.
explanations.D: Social reproduction describes persistence of social advantage across generations.

questions.stem: How might urge surfing reduce vaping-related behavior?

choices.B: It trains noticing cravings without immediately responding to them
choices.A: It removes nicotine from the bloodstream instantly
choices.C: It makes delayed rewards physically larger
choices.D: It prevents all future cravings permanently

explanations.B: Correct. The mindfulness group reported observing urges without acting on them and showed lower urge ratings. This fits the idea that cravings can be tolerated as temporary experiences rather than automatically triggering use.
explanations.A: The exercise is psychological and does not instantly change nicotine levels.
explanations.C: The monetary rewards did not change; the choices changed.
explanations.D: The passage describes short-term urge management, not permanent elimination of craving.

questions.stem: A counselor applying the passage would most likely teach which skill?

choices.C: Recognizing cravings as transient sensations while delaying action
choices.B: Telling clients that urges prove failure
choices.D: Encouraging immediate vaping whenever urges appear

explanations.C: Correct. The passage supports a coping strategy that separates craving from action. Teaching clients to observe urges and wait before responding targets the impulsive link between craving and use.
explanations.A: Punishment for reporting cravings could reduce honesty and support, not self-regulation.
explanations.B: Cravings are common during behavior change and do not prove failure.
explanations.D: Immediate use is the behavior the intervention is trying to reduce.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is most justified by the study?

choices.A: The mindfulness exercise was associated with lower urge ratings and less immediate-reward choosing in this task
choices.C: Budgeting exercises are harmful in all contexts

explanations.A: Correct. The study supports a short-term effect on urge ratings and delay-discounting choices within the experimental task. It does not justify claims about permanent cure or broad condemnation of the other tasks.
explanations.B: The passage does not measure permanent abstinence or cure.
explanations.C: The budgeting exercise was simply a comparison task and is not shown to be broadly harmful.
explanations.D: The reading task was a control condition, not evidence of causing addiction.
